content:
Harden the outline-decorations implementation now on `main`. This is the productionization pass for the decoration system that came out of the experiments (additive indentation + CSS-gradient guides + SVG icon block markers) — polish and robustness only, no new features. Work through the SDD loop: the authoritative work list is the remaining unchecked tasks in `openspec/changes/outline-decorations/tasks.md` (the hardening section, plus any mobile findings recorded there by the mobile-testing PR's triage) — use `/openspec-apply-change` to drive it. Branch off `main`, draft PR back to `main`, one commit per task or coherent task group, checking tasks off as they land.

**Read before touching anything**: `docs/research/11-decoration-lessons.md` — every entry is a mistake already made once in this codebase, and several directly constrain this work (never append DOM children into a `.cm-line` — confirmed 100%+ CPU feedback loop via CM6's mutation observer; measure the chevron's `.collapse-indicator.collapse-icon` glyph, not its zero-width `.cm-fold-indicator` wrapper; when a box-widening value drives multiple painted layers, compute it once and thread it everywhere; matching beats escalating in cascade fights; read native values live instead of hardcoding). Then `docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md`'s "Next steps: hardening 5a" section for each task's full rationale, and the openspec change's `design.md` for the architecture as specced.

Guidance on the known tasks (defer to tasks.md where it says more):

- **Fold-chevron live measurement**: replace the two hardcoded constants in the static CSS transform (`0.425rem` half-wrapper-width, `3px` dead space) with a live-measured value, following the pattern `MarginCompensation` already establishes for `nativeMarginBasePx` (measure one representative element per render, apply uniformly). Keep the behavior the experiments validated: the reach applies to headings unconditionally (the chevron gutter is reserved even before a heading has children — markers must not jump sideways when a first child is added).
- **`app.workspace.updateOptions()` vs. the forceRedraw hack**: evaluate before replacing. The bar: after changing `markerVisibility` on a note containing *only* widget-replaced atoms (a lone table — the exact case where decoration output is byte-identical and CM6 never re-fires `docViewUpdate`), the marker updates without a mode toggle. If `updateOptions()` demonstrably passes that scenario, replace the off/on toggle; if not, document why and keep the hack. Either way the table-only scenario keeps a regression test.
- **Parse consolidation**: three ViewPlugins currently each run `parse()`/`decorate()` per transaction. Consolidate into one shared computation feeding all consumers — without breaking the `isNestedEditor` gating, the StateField/ViewPlugin split for widget atoms, or the plain-line vs. widget marker mechanisms. This is a refactor of working, heavily-tested code: the full suite is the safety net, and no behavior change is expected or acceptable.
- **Declarative settings API**: adopt `getSettingDefinitions()` on the settings tab (the one remaining lint warning; Obsidian 1.13+ settings-search support). Consult current Obsidian API docs/typings rather than guessing the shape.
- **Accessibility/polish**: `aria-hidden="true"` on marker SVGs. Then verification passes (not necessarily fixes) for RTL text, IME composition at line start, and 2–3 community themes: record findings as new tasks or a research-doc note, fixing only what's cheap and safe — same feedback-loop framing as mobile.
- **Explicitly out of scope**: the "shrinking only our own added list margin" open question stays undecided — it's a design decision to surface, not implement. If any task turns out to require a genuine design choice, stop and surface it rather than deciding unilaterally.

**Verification bar — the project's own hard rules, not suggestions**: green tests alone prove nothing about rendering. For every visual-surface change: `npm run build:plugin` (plain `npm run build` only type-checks and has produced false verification twice), screenshot the full fixture corpus (`ALL_DECORATION_FIXTURES`) in both bundled themes, and run the full desktop e2e suite plus `npm run test:e2e:mobile` (mobile is a feasibility feedback loop, not a gate — triage failures per the established buckets: test artifact → fix here; real mobile rendering issue → record as task; architectural blocker → research-doc addendum). Pixel/computed-style assertions over DOM-attribute checks. Lint must stay at zero errors and must not regress the marker code. A real-vault pass in a live Obsidian instance remains the final bar for anything visual — flag explicitly in the PR description which changes need that manual pass, since it can't be automated.

Any doc/spec text you touch: neutral team voice (we/our, "manual review"/"a tester"; never you/your or session-log phrasing). Update the corresponding checklist item statuses in `docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md` as tasks land. If every hardening task completes, do NOT archive the openspec change or run sync-specs — note readiness in the PR description and leave that as a deliberate follow-up decision.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## c9886e0d-2fc9-452f-a984-23dcb04932ed
DeepWiki MCP provides AI-powered documentation for GitHub repositories.

Available tools:
- read_wiki_structure: Get a list of documentation topics for a repository
- read_wiki_contents: View full documentation about a repository
- ask_question: Ask any question about a repository and get an AI-powered answer
- list_available_repos: List your available repositories (private mode only)
- generate_wiki: Generate a codebase wiki for a repository — only use when explicitly requested by the user (private mode only)
- devin_automation_manage: Manage Devin automations — list, get, create, update, delete, or fetch the trigger event schemas (private mode only)
- devin_code_scan_manage: Manage Devin code scans — list scans, list findings, list profiles, get a profile (private mode only)
- devin_knowledge_manage: Manage Devin knowledge notes and suggestions — list, search, get, create, update, delete notes, view folder structure, list/view/dismiss knowledge suggestions (private mode only)
- devin_playbook_manage: Manage Devin playbooks — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_schedule_manage: Manage scheduled Devin sessions — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_session_create: Create one or more child Devin sessions (private mode only)
- devin_session_interact: Manage a Devin session — get status, send messages, sleep/terminate/archive, read messages & attachments, manage tags (private mode only)
- devin_session_events: Inspect session events — list summaries, fetch full details, or search event contents (private mode only)
- devin_session_search: Search and filter Devin sessions (private mode only)
- list_integrations: List all native integrations and MCP servers with their status and settings URLs (private mode only)
- find_setting: Find Devin webapp settings pages and deep-link URLs for them (private mode only)

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

attachment.content:
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- openspec-apply-change: Implement tasks from an OpenSpec change. Use when the user wants to start implementing, continue implementation, or work through tasks.
- openspec-archive-change: Archive a completed change in the experimental workflow. Use when the user wants to finalize and archive a change after implementation is complete.
- openspec-explore: Enter explore mode - a thinking partner for exploring ideas, investigating problems, and clarifying requirements. Use when the user wants to think through something before or during a change.
- openspec-propose: Propose a new change with all artifacts generated in one step. Use when the user wants to quickly describe what they want to build and get a complete proposal with design, specs, and tasks ready for implementation.
- openspec-sync-specs: Sync delta specs from a change to main specs. Use when the user wants to update main specs with changes from a delta spec, without archiving the change.
- openspec-update-change: Update an OpenSpec change by revising its existing planning artifacts and keeping them coherent with one another. Use when the user wants to revise a change's plan, fold new decisions into it, or reconcile its artifacts after an edit. Never edits code.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:doc-coauthoring: Guide users through a structured workflow for co-authoring documentation. Use when user wants to write documentation, proposals, technical specs, decision docs, or similar structured content. This workflow helps users efficiently transfer context, refine content through iteration, and verify the doc works for readers. Trigger when user mentions writing docs, creating proposals, drafting specs, or similar documentation tasks.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:mct-cbt-therapy: AI-assisted therapy integrating Metacognitive Therapy (MCT) and Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT). Use when conducting therapeutic conversations, providing mental health support, or when user seeks help with anxiety, depression, worry, rumination, negative thought patterns, or emotional distress. Triggers on therapy-related requests, mental health discussions, or explicit requests for MCT/CBT techniques.
- anthropic-skills:morning: Render the user's morning brief as a styled HTML artifact, or set it up as a recurring weekday task. Use when: morning brief, daily brief, set up my morning brief, what's my day look like, run my brief.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:phenomenology-guide: Comparative contemplative philosophy guide for understanding experiences in meditation and daily life. Use when user asks "what is this state?", "why does X happen in practice?", "what did tradition Y believe about Z?", wants to understand contemplative phenomena, seeks connections between traditions, or wants to learn history of philosophy, theology, and traditional metaphysics alongside naturalized interpretations. Maps experiences to both scientific mechanisms and traditional frameworks without forcing metaphysical commitment.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:practice-architecture: Design and refine personal contemplative practice systems. Use when user wants to build a practice routine, asks "what should I practice?", needs help sequencing practices, feels scattered across techniques, wants to develop consistency, struggles with habit discipline, or asks about integrating different traditions. Provides structure while respecting individual constraints and preferences. Emphasizes minimal viable practice over ambitious programs that collapse.
- anthropic-skills:reactive-pattern: Multi-layer intervention for intense reactive patterns—rumination loops, anxiety spirals, emotional flooding, obsessive thought trains. Use when user is caught in a strong pattern that standard techniques haven't touched, when they're spinning in their head, when emotions are overwhelming, or when they need in-the-moment guidance beyond thought records. Integrates somatic state assessment, nervous system regulation, feeling-tone awareness (vedanā), Stoic impression testing, and MCT detached mindfulness.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:somatic-navigator: Body-based nervous system regulation and interoceptive awareness guide. Use when user needs to regulate their physiological state, wants to understand their nervous system, is too activated for cognitive approaches, experiences anxiety/panic, feels shutdown/numb/frozen, asks about breathwork or body-based practices, or wants to develop body awareness. Provides specific protocols with timing and physiological rationale—neurobiology, not mysticism.
- anthropic-skills:values-clarity: Socratic dialogue for exploring what matters, clarifying personal values, and addressing values-action gaps. Use when user questions life direction, feels meaningless or stuck, experiences conflict between what they do and what matters to them, wants to understand their priorities, or asks about purpose, meaning, or "what should I do with my life." Integrates ACT values work, Stoic virtue ethics, and naturalized Buddhist ethics without metaphysical baggage.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/laughedelic/Code/obsidian-true-outliner/.claude/skills/openspec-apply-change

Implement tasks from an OpenSpec change.

**Store selection:** If the user names a store (a store is a standalone OpenSpec repo registered on this machine) or the work lives in one, run `openspec store list --json` to discover registered store ids, then pass `--store <id>` on the commands that read or write specs and changes (`new change`, `status`, `instructions`, `list`, `show`, `validate`, `archive`, `doctor`, `context`). Other commands do not take the flag. Hints printed by commands already carry the flag; keep it on follow-ups. Without a store, commands act on the nearest local `openspec/` root.

**Input**: Optionally specify a change name. If omitted, check if it can be inferred from conversation context. If vague or ambiguous you MUST prompt for available changes.

**Steps**

1. **Select the change**

   If a name is provided, use it. Otherwise:
   - Infer from conversation context if the user mentioned a change
   - Auto-select if only one active change exists
   - If ambiguous, run `openspec list --json` to get available changes and use the **AskUserQuestion tool** to let the user select

   Always announce: "Using change: <name>" and how to override (e.g., `/opsx:apply <other>`).

2. **Check status to understand the schema**
   ```bash
   openspec status --change "<name>" --json
   ```
   Parse the JSON to understand:
   - `schemaName`: The workflow being used (e.g., "spec-driven")
   - `planningHome`, `changeRoot`, and `actionContext`: planning scope and edit constraints
   - Which artifact contains the tasks (typically "tasks" for spec-driven, check status for others)

3. **Get apply instructions**

   ```bash
   openspec instructions apply --change "<name>" --json
   ```

   This returns:
   - `contextFiles`: artifact ID -> array of concrete file paths (varies by schema - could be proposal/specs/design/tasks or spec/tests/implementation/docs)
   - Progress (total, complete, remaining)
   - Task list with status
   - Dynamic instruction based on current state

   **Handle states:**
   - If `state: "blocked"` (missing artifacts): show message, suggest using openspec-continue-change
   - If `state: "all_done"`: congratulate, suggest archive
   - Otherwise: proceed to implementation

4. **Read context files**

   Read every file path listed under `contextFiles` from the apply instructions output.
   The files depend on the schema being used:
   - **spec-driven**: proposal, specs, design, tasks
   - Other schemas: follow the contextFiles from CLI output

5. **Show current progress**

   Display:
   - Schema being used
   - Progress: "N/M tasks complete"
   - Remaining tasks overview
   - Dynamic instruction from CLI

6. **Implement tasks (loop until done or blocked)**

   For each pending task:
   - Show which task is being worked on
   - Make the code changes required
   - Keep changes minimal and focused
   - Mark task complete in the tasks file: `- [ ]` → `- [x]`
   - Continue to next task

   **Pause if:**
   - Task is unclear → ask for clarification
   - Implementation reveals a design issue → suggest updating artifacts
   - Error or blocker encountered → report and wait for guidance
   - User interrupts

7. **On completion or pause, show status**

   Display:
   - Tasks completed this session
   - Overall progress: "N/M tasks complete"
   - If all done: suggest archive
   - If paused: explain why and wait for guidance

**Output During Implementation**

```
## Implementing: <change-name> (schema: <schema-name>)

Working on task 3/7: <task description>
[...implementation happening...]
✓ Task complete

Working on task 4/7: <task description>
[...implementation happening...]
✓ Task complete
```

**Output On Completion**

```
## Implementation Complete

**Change:** <change-name>
**Schema:** <schema-name>
**Progress:** 7/7 tasks complete ✓

### Completed This Session
- [x] Task 1
- [x] Task 2
...

All tasks complete! Ready to archive this change.
```

**Output On Pause (Issue Encountered)**

```
## Implementation Paused

**Change:** <change-name>
**Schema:** <schema-name>
**Progress:** 4/7 tasks complete

### Issue Encountered
<description of the issue>

**Options:**
1. <option 1>
2. <option 2>
3. Other approach

What would you like to do?
```

**Guardrails**
- Keep going through tasks until done or blocked
- Always read context files before starting (from the apply instructions output)
- If task is ambiguous, pause and ask before implementing
- If implementation reveals issues, pause and suggest artifact updates
- Keep code changes minimal and scoped to each task
- Update task checkbox immediately after completing each task
- Pause on errors, blockers, or unclear requirements - don't guess
- Use contextFiles from CLI output, don't assume specific file names

**Fluid Workflow Integration**

This skill supports the "actions on a change" model:

- **Can be invoked anytime**: Before all artifacts are done (if tasks exist), after partial implementation, interleaved with other actions
- **Allows artifact updates**: If implementation reveals design issues, suggest updating artifacts - not phase-locked, work fluidly


ARGUMENTS: outline-decorations

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service — even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer — your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## deepwiki
DeepWiki MCP provides AI-powered documentation for GitHub repositories.

Available tools:
- read_wiki_structure: Get a list of documentation topics for a repository
- read_wiki_contents: View full documentation about a repository
- ask_question: Ask any question about a repository and get an AI-powered answer
- list_available_repos: List your available repositories (private mode only)
- generate_wiki: Generate a codebase wiki for a repository — only use when explicitly requested by the user (private mode only)
- devin_automation_manage: Manage Devin automations — list, get, create, update, delete, or fetch the trigger event schemas (private mode only)
- devin_code_scan_manage: Manage Devin code scans — list scans, list findings, list profiles, get a profile (private mode only)
- devin_knowledge_manage: Manage Devin knowledge notes and suggestions — list, search, get, create, update, delete notes, view folder structure, list/view/dismiss knowledge suggestions (private mode only)
- devin_playbook_manage: Manage Devin playbooks — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_schedule_manage: Manage scheduled Devin sessions — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_session_create: Create one or more child Devin sessions (private mode only)
- devin_session_interact: Manage a Devin session — get status, send messages, sleep/terminate/archive, read messages & attachments, manage tags (private mode only)
- devin_session_events: Inspect session events — list summaries, fetch full details, or search event contents (private mode only)
- devin_session_search: Search and filter Devin sessions (private mode only)
- list_integrations: List all native integrations and MCP servers with their status and settings URLs (private mode only)
- find_setting: Find Devin webapp settings pages and deep-link URLs for them (private mode only)

7	- [x] 0.1 Experiment 1: additive-only indentation — **kept**, merged (PR #8). Details:
8	      [08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md](../../../docs/research/08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md)
9	- [x] 0.2 Experiment 2a: guide lines via pixel-measured overlay — fully capable, superseded
10	      by 2b, closed unmerged (PR #9). Details:
11	      [09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md](../../../docs/research/09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md)
12	- [x] 0.3 Experiment 2b: guide lines via CSS stacked-gradient — **chosen**, merged (PR #10)
13	- [x] 0.4 Experiment 3 (minimal marker fallback): not triggered — the marker question was
14	      later answered by Experiment 5 instead; see doc 07's own results table
15	- [x] 0.5 Experiment 4 (widget-spacer risk-spike): not triggered — no fragility observed
16	      that would have called for it
17	- [x] 0.6 This task: comparison table filled in (doc 07), winning techniques picked (1, 2b,
18	      5a), design.md/spec.md/tasks.md (this file) rewritten with validated decisions
19	
20	Experiment 5 (per-kind block markers, added after 0.6 was originally scoped, run to the
21	same discipline): 5a (SVG icons, DOM mechanism) **chosen**, merged (PR #11/#12); 5b (CSS
22	shapes → uniform dot) closed unmerged (PR #13), full account and head-to-head verdict in
23	[10-experiment-5-block-markers.md](../../../docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md).
24	Cross-experiment lessons:
25	[11-decoration-lessons.md](../../../docs/research/11-decoration-lessons.md).
26	
27	## 1. Depth/marker computation (pure module) — done
28	
29	- [x] 1.1 `src/plugin/decorate.ts`: `decorate(doc)` computes per-line
30	  `{ lineNumber, depth, isFirstLine, hasNativeMarker, isAtom, isListItem,
31	  supplementalDepth, kind, hasChildren }`, walking the tree in document order (own lines,
32	  then children). `computeLineGuides(doc)` computes per-line `{ lineNumber, guideDepths,
33	  isGapLine }` in a separate single-pass walk, covering blank `trailingGap` lines too (a
34	  strict superset of `decorate()`'s coverage). No CM6 imports; both unit-tested directly
35	  against parsed fixtures.
36	- [x] 1.2 Unit tests (`tests/decorate.test.ts`, 32 cases): heading/list/paragraph-adjacency
37	  depth agreement, gap-line handling, multiline continuation, empty/preamble-only documents,
38	  `supplementalDepth`'s byte-identical-pure-list invariant, `kind`/`hasChildren` tracking for
39	  the marker layer, and the full `computeLineGuides` suite (bridging, exclusion, gap
40	  continuity, superset coverage). See the spec's per-requirement "Covered by" notes for the
41	  exact mapping.
42	
43	## 2. CM6 decoration extension — done
44	
45	- [x] 2.1 `src/plugin/decorations.ts`: three `ViewPlugin`s (`DecorationsPlugin` for
46	  indentation + guides, `MarkersPlugin` for the plain-line marker widget, `MarginCompensation`
47	  for widget-atom DOM patching and native-base-margin compensation), each gated on
48	  `DecorationSource` (`ModeSource` + `markerVisibility`) and each checking `isNestedEditor(view)`
49	  before computing. Recomputes on every `ViewUpdate` (not just `docChanged`), since toggling
50	  outline mode has no document change of its own.
51	- [x] 2.2 `styles.css`: `--to-depth`/`--to-guides` custom properties consumed by `calc()` and
52	  a shared `::after` gradient stack; marker gutter reservation; `contain`/`overflow`
53	  overrides for widget atoms (matching Obsidian's own selector specificity); fold-chevron
54	  repositioning. Verified against bundled light/dark themes and (guides) one community theme.
55	- [x] 2.3 Registered in `main.ts` via `registerEditorExtension`, alongside `grammarExtension`.
56	
57	## 3. Verification — done for the chosen path
58	
59	- [x] 3.1 Full gate green: `tsc`, `lint` (0 errors; 1 pre-existing warning unrelated to
60	  decorations — see section 4), `vitest`, plugin build — reverified after each experiment's
61	  merge.
62	- [x] 3.2 `e2e/specs/`: `50-decorations.e2e.ts` (indentation, additive invariant, widget
63	  atoms, fold-indicator non-collision), `51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts` (guide bridging/exclusion,
64	  gap continuity, blockquote/table coexistence, native-base-margin composition),
65	  `52-block-markers-icons.e2e.ts` (per-kind markers, visibility setting, fold-chevron
66	  clearance, size invariance) — see spec.md's per-requirement "Covered by" notes for the
67	  full mapping.
68	- [x] 3.3 Manual/visual residue: theme sweep done per-experiment (bundled light/dark
69	  throughout; one community theme for guides). No single consolidated `verification.md`
70	  entry was added — recorded instead in each experiment's own results doc (08 §Results, 09
71	  §Results, 10 §Results/head-to-head). Consider consolidating into `verification.md` in a
72	  future pass if that split proves hard to navigate.
73	- [x] 3.4 Dev-vault visual pass: done per-experiment against the bundled `test-vault`'s real
74	  notes (journal/notes/README), screenshotted both themes, reviewed by eye each round — see
75	  each experiment doc's "real-vault" findings. The original open-questions.md motivating
76	  finding (flat paragraph-heavy documents give no visual signal) is resolved: indentation +
77	  guides alone give real hierarchy legibility even before a marker is added (08's "also
78	  confirmed not a bug" note); markers add per-kind identification on top.
79	  **Not yet done**: a pass by the plugin author's own hand, in their own personal Obsidian
80	  vault, on the final merged (1+2b+5a) combination together — every experiment's own
81	  "real-vault" pass used the bundled `test-vault` proxy or, per doc 10, review of the
82	  bundled `test-vault`'s content through the e2e harness, not the author's live instance.
83	  Doc 10 flags this explicitly as the "stronger bar" still outstanding.
84	
85	## 4. Traceability gaps found during the 0.6 backfill
86	
87	Requirements in `specs/outline-decorations/spec.md` with no direct covering test (recorded
88	so they don't silently stay uncovered):
89	
90	- [ ] 4.1 **Nested per-cell editor decoration state** has no dedicated e2e test. The only
91	  evidence is indirect: a marker-visibility test's intermittent failure was traced to this
92	  exact leak and is now passing deterministically, but no test explicitly opens a table cell
93	  for editing and asserts the nested editor's own line carries no padding/margin/marker. Add
94	  one to `e2e/specs/52-block-markers-icons.e2e.ts` (or a new nested-editor-focused spec).
95	- [ ] 4.2 **The non-mutation contract** (no transaction/cursor/undo-stack side effect from a
96	  decoration recompute) has no dedicated e2e assertion — only inferable from the pure
97	  module's own lack of side effects and the absence of any reported regression. Add an e2e
98	  test that snapshots cursor position and undo-stack depth before/after a decoration
99	  recompute (e.g. triggered by a mode toggle) and asserts both are unchanged.
100	
101	Code behaviors present in `decorations.ts` with no explicit spec requirement (recorded as
102	either intentionally-undocumented implementation detail or a genuine gap — reviewed and
103	judged not to need their own requirement, since they're implementation strategies for
104	requirements already stated, not separately-observable behavior):
105	
106	- Kept as implementation detail, not spec'd: the specific choice of two `ViewPlugin`s
107	  (`DecorationsPlugin`/`MarkersPlugin`) vs. one merged one — the spec cares about the
108	  observable outcome (guides + markers both render correctly together), not this
109	  implementation split.
110	- Kept as implementation detail: `MarginCompensation`'s idempotent DOM-patch skip (avoiding
111	  a rebuild when kind/position haven't changed) — a performance detail, not user-observable
112	  behavior.
113	
114	## 5. Remaining work: hardening 5a for production
115	
116	Ranked, from
117	[docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md](../../../docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md)'s
118	"Next steps: hardening 5a" section. None are architecture-threatening.
119	
120	- [ ] 5.1 Replace the two hardcoded fold-chevron measurement constants
121	  (`0.425rem`/`3px` in the chevron-repositioning `translateX`) with live measurement,
122	  porting 5b's approach (`getBoundingClientRect()` against the chevron's own glyph, not its
123	  wrapper). Currently the one place this design violates its own "read native values live"
124	  rule; a theme/Obsidian update that resizes the chevron silently degrades layout
125	  (cosmetic-only failure mode).
126	- [ ] 5.2 Protect two documented invariants in code review going forward: (a) DOM injection
127	  into widget-atom subtrees relies on Obsidian never re-diffing those opaque subtrees
128	  internally — undocumented-to-Obsidian, failure mode is re-injection flicker/duplicated
129	  markers; (b) never append a child into a plain `.cm-line` — confirmed to peg CM6's
130	  renderer at 100%+ CPU via its mutation-observer feedback loop (already documented inline
131	  in `decorations.ts`'s module comment; this task is about keeping it true across future
132	  refactors, e.g. by adding a lint rule or a regression test if a lightweight one is
133	  feasible).
134	- [ ] 5.3 Evaluate `app.workspace.updateOptions()` as a replacement for the `forceRedraw`
135	  off/on mode-toggle hack currently used to refresh `MarginCompensation` after a settings
136	  change when decoration output is byte-identical (e.g. `markerVisibility` changing on a
137	  table-only note). The hack works and its reasoning is sound, but toggling a user-visible
138	  mode as an internal refresh mechanism is fragile if mode toggling ever gains side effects.
139	  `updateOptions()` is Obsidian's public API for "editor-extension-affecting settings
140	  changed"; obsidian-lapel confirms the pattern works in the wild.
141	- [ ] 5.4 Consolidate to one shared `parse()`/`decorate()` pass per transaction. Currently
142	  three `ViewPlugin`s each independently re-parse the full document on every update (the 2b
143	  baseline already did it twice; 5a added a third). Same asymptotics, tripled constant —
144	  fine for normal notes, worth doing before testing against multi-thousand-line files.
145	  Building only over `view.viewport` (rebuild on `docChanged || viewportChanged`) is a
146	  further, separately-deferred option — obsidian-lapel demonstrates the standard shape, and
147	  additionally shows CM6's own incremental `syntaxTree` could supply per-line *kind*
148	  classification with no separate reparse, though not our tree *depths* (our universal-tree
149	  semantics aren't in CM6's grammar).
150	- [ ] 5.5 Adopt the declarative settings API (`getSettingDefinitions`) for
151	  `TrueOutlinerSettingTab` in `main.ts` — the one remaining `npm run lint` warning as of this
152	  backfill (`obsidianmd/settings-tab/prefer-setting-definitions`; confirmed 0 errors, 1
153	  warning on `main`). Split out as its own task rather than bundled with 5.6, since the
154	  `eslint-plugin-obsidianmd` *errors* Experiment 5a originally introduced (inline styles
155	  tripping `no-static-styles-assignment`/`prefer-create-el`) are already resolved on `main`
156	  — see the corresponding status note in doc 10.
157	- [ ] 5.6 Polish: `aria-hidden="true"` on the marker SVGs (decorative, screen readers should
158	  skip them); untested contexts — RTL text, IME composition at line start, community themes
159	  beyond those already exercised (bundled light/dark, one community theme for guides only).
160	- [x] 5.7 Mobile-emulation verification pass — done (PR #7, "test: automate mobile smoke
161	  testing via Obsidian mobile emulation"). This is a feedback loop for continuously
162	  assessing mobile feasibility, not a hard mobile-support requirement — full mobile support
163	  isn't a goal at this stage; the project's standing bar remains "mobile-safe from day 1,
164	  desktop-tested for v1.0" (docs/research/04-open-questions.md Q7). The value is early
165	  discovery: if a design or architecture choice here would make mobile support harder or
166	  impossible later, we want that insight now, while it's cheap to react to, rather than once
167	  mobile becomes the focus.
168	
169	  Ran the full decoration corpus (`50-decorations.e2e.ts`, `51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts`,
170	  `52-block-markers-icons.e2e.ts`) plus the real-vault-equivalent screenshots, under
171	  Obsidian's mobile emulation (390×844 viewport) against the merged 1 → 2b → 5a
172	  implementation — never previously exercised under mobile emulation. Result: no
173	  mobile-specific rendering issues found. Indentation, guides, and markers all render
174	  correctly at the narrow viewport; the "mobile-safe" goal (CM6 `Decoration`/CSS/DOM only, no
175	  Node/Electron APIs) holds under emulation, consistent with `no-nodejs-modules` already
176	  enforcing it statically. One local-machine artifact was observed and ruled out during this
177	  pass: screenshot-heavy tests in `50-decorations.e2e.ts` timed out (chromedriver render
178	  timeouts) when run as part of the full suite on a memory-pressured dev machine, but passed
179	  cleanly in three separate isolated reruns against the same code — attributed to session
180	  degradation from local system load, not a defect; confirmed clean on a fresh CI runner.
181	
182	  Not covered by this pass: the real Capacitor mobile app (emulation is still Electron under
183	  a phone-sized viewport, so it can't surface a platform gap that isn't viewport/CSS) — see
184	  README's "Mobile testing" section for the real-Android/iOS coverage gap and options.
185	- [ ] 5.8 Accepted design costs, restated so they aren't rediscovered as bugs: every
186	  non-list line reserves a 1.25rem marker gutter, so text visibly shifts when toggling
187	  outline mode; two Experiment-1 invariants were knowingly relaxed (depth-0 lines are no
188	  longer padding-free; same-depth list items vs. atoms no longer share a column). The
189	  pure-list invariant (spec.md's "A pure list renders byte-identical to outline-mode-off")
190	  still holds unconditionally.
191	
192	## 6. Open question (not yet decided, not a committed task)
193	
194	**Shrinking only our own added list margin.** Raised during Experiment 1's review. List
195	items sit visibly further right than a same-depth sibling paragraph/blockquote — this is
196	Obsidian's own native list hang (`text-indent`/`padding-left`), present in vanilla Obsidian
197	regardless of outline mode, not a regression this design introduces. A narrower,
198	still-additive-only fix stays open as a possible follow-up: reduce only the margin *we* add*
199	to list items by the list root's own native hang width, read live via `getComputedStyle`
200	(the same technique Experiment 1's table-padding fix already established). Two risks noted
201	but unresolved before attempting it: (a) clamping so a shallow `supplementalDepth` never
202	goes negative once the hang is subtracted; (b) the compensation must be based on the list
203	**root**'s own hang, not each item's, since nested items can have wider markers (e.g. `10.`
204	vs. `-`) with different native hang widths — compensating per-item would reintroduce the
205	kind of within-list misalignment the wide-numbering fixture exists to catch. See
206	[docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md](../../../docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md#open-question-shrinking-only-our-own-added-list-margin)
207	for the full framing. Not scheduled; revisit only on deliberate follow-up, not as part of
208	the hardening checklist above.

## 1. Depth/marker computation (pure module) — done
