/*
 * Outline mode decorations — additive-only indentation (Experiment 1, see
 * docs/research/08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md and the postmortem
 * it links). `rem`, not `em`: custom properties resolve at the point of use,
 * so an `em`-based unit would inflate under a heading's larger font-size —
 * exactly the bug that shipped last time.
 *
 * Selector specificity: `!important` alone is not enough. Obsidian's own
 * app.css has `.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > * { margin: 0px
 * !important; }` (three classes) resetting margin on every `.cm-line` —
 * our `.cm-line.to-decor-*` (two classes) lost to it outright even with
 * `!important` on both sides, confirmed live against a real build. Matching
 * the same ancestor structure they use is what wins the cascade, not the
 * `!important` keyword by itself.
 */

/*
 * `--to-marker-gutter` (Experiment 5a, see
 * docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md): a fixed, additive
 * reservation for a per-kind block marker icon, on top of the existing
 * depth-based indentation. Applies to every non-list-item line (block and
 * atom alike) — list items reserve nothing, since they keep their fully
 * native marker untouched. `rem`, never `em`, same reasoning as
 * `--to-decor-unit` above. Explicitly set per-line by `lineDecoration()`
 * (decorations.ts) — this rule's own fallback (`0px`) only matters before
 * that JS has run at all; it is never the value actually in effect.
 * `position: relative` gives the marker icon
 * (a real, absolutely-positioned DOM child, not a pseudo-element — the
 * whole reason Experiment 5a doesn't need to fight over `::before`/
 * `::after` with the guide or blockquote's native bar) a containing block
 * matching this line's own box.
 */
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-block {
  position: relative;
  padding-left: calc(
    var(--to-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem) + var(--to-marker-gutter, 0px)
  ) !important;
}

/* Atoms (code/table/quote/callout/html/hr) render a visible background or
 * border box; padding-left only shifts an element's own content, never
 * that box, so atoms need margin-left instead. */
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-atom {
  position: relative;
  margin-left: calc(
    var(--to-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem) + var(--to-marker-gutter, 0px)
  ) !important;
}

/* List items: native text-indent/padding-left (the bullet hang) is never
 * touched. margin-left is a box-model property native list rendering
 * doesn't otherwise use, so adding supplemental depth here is genuinely
 * additive on top of untouched native positioning. */
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-list {
  margin-left: calc(var(--to-supp-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
}

/*
 * Guide lines (Experiment 2b) — CSS stacked-gradient technique
 * (@replit/codemirror-indentation-markers), see
 * docs/research/09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md. decorations.ts merges a
 * `--to-guides` custom property (a comma-joined list of repeating-linear-
 * gradient layers, one per active ancestor depth) into the same line that
 * already carries `--to-depth`/`--to-supp-depth` — a single `::before`
 * renders every layer as one background, O(1) DOM nodes regardless of
 * depth, on every kind (block, atom, list item, AND widget-replaced atoms
 * — table/callout/hr/html — which AtomWidgetMargins patches directly via
 * the same class/custom-properties, since a `Decoration.line` has no
 * effect on those at all, same as its margin-left compensation).
 *
 * `to-decor-atom`/`to-decor-list` lines use `margin-left`, which shifts
 * the LINE'S OWN BOX — an early version of this code assumed that meant a
 * guide could never render on those lines at all (a background confined
 * to match the line's own box would indeed be stuck there). That was
 * wrong: a pseudo-element's own box does NOT have to match its containing
 * block's dimensions. `left` here widens the pseudo leftward by exactly
 * `--to-own-shift` — the line's own fully-known margin-left shift — so
 * its box starts at the SAME global column regardless of which kind of
 * line it's on, and `background-position` inside decorations.ts's
 * gradient layers then lands each guide at the correct absolute column.
 * Confirmed live that nothing between a plain `.cm-line` and
 * `.cm-scroller` clips this leftward overflow.
 *
 * Neither `!important` nor Obsidian's own ancestor-chain specificity
 * workaround is needed for `position`/`background` themselves (unlike
 * padding-left/margin-left above): those aren't contested by any of
 * Obsidian's own rules on `.cm-line`/`.cm-embed-block` — confirmed live.
 *
 * Uses `::after`, not `::before`: Obsidian implements a blockquote's own
 * colored side-bar via a NATIVE `::before` (`border-left`, `position:
 * absolute; left: 0`, confirmed live via computed style with outline mode
 * off) — an early version of this rule used `::before` too, which doesn't
 * "double up" with the native one, it completely REPLACES it (an element
 * has exactly one `::before`), silently deleting the native bar wherever
 * a guide became active on a blockquote line. Confirmed `::after` is
 * unused (`content: none`) by every kind this rule targets, including all
 * four widget kinds, before switching to it.
 */
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-guides,
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-embed-block.to-decor-guides {
  position: relative;
}

.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-guides::after,
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-embed-block.to-decor-guides::after {
  content: '';
  position: absolute;
  top: 0;
  left: calc(-1 * var(--to-own-shift, 0px));
  right: 0;
  bottom: 0;
  background: var(--to-guides);
  opacity: 0.6;
  pointer-events: none;
  z-index: -1;
}

/*
 * Widget-replaced atoms (table/callout/html/hr) DO need one more override,
 * and it's a genuinely different mechanism from the padding/margin
 * cascade fight above: Obsidian's own app.css sets `contain: paint
 * !important` on `.cm-content > [contenteditable="false"]` (confirmed via
 * live inspection of matching stylesheet rules) — likely a rendering/
 * virtualization isolation hint for these widgets. Paint containment
 * clips ALL descendant painting (including a pseudo-element) to the
 * element's own box, REGARDLESS of `overflow` — a plain `.cm-line` never
 * has this, which is why atoms/list items above needed no equivalent
 * rule. `!important` alone isn't enough to beat it (same lesson as the
 * padding/margin fight): a 2-class override lost on specificity until it
 * matched Obsidian's own 3-selector chain, confirmed live via a bisection
 * test (a probe `::before` was completely invisible at any negative
 * `left`, even a single pixel, until specificity was matched — ruling out
 * a z-index/stacking explanation, since the SAME probe rendered fine at a
 * non-negative offset with no `contain` override at all).
 *
 * Confirmed safe to remove: paint containment is a pure rendering
 * isolation/perf hint with no effect on visual output by itself: removing
 * it changes nothing else about how callout/hr/html render (verified by
 * screenshot).
 *
 * Table needed one more fix on top of `contain`: it also carries its own
 * `overflow-x: auto` on this SAME outer element (for horizontal scrolling
 * of wide tables). Forcing `overflow: visible` here too naively (tried and
 * measured live) makes the table's own wide content spill out unclipped —
 * and since nothing else up the ancestor chain clips it either, that width
 * cascades all the way to `.cm-scroller`, turning the WHOLE document
 * horizontally scrollable instead of just the table (confirmed live: the
 * rest of the note visibly scrolled out of view together with the table).
 * CSS overflow is all-or-nothing per box — there's no way to say "clip my
 * own rightward overflow but let this pseudo-element bleed left."
 *
 * Fix: Obsidian's own table widget conveniently already wraps the actual
 * `<table>` (plus its add-row/add-column buttons) in an inner
 * `.table-wrapper` div, separate from the outer element this rule targets.
 * Moving `overflow-x: auto` onto `.table-wrapper` instead decouples the two
 * concerns onto two different boxes: the OUTER element stays
 * `overflow: visible` (letting the guide's `::after` bleed left, as below),
 * while `.table-wrapper` independently scrolls/clips the actual wide table
 * content. Verified via computed style + a programmatic `scrollLeft` write
 * that this restores real scrolling (outer no longer overflows at all;
 * wrapper does, and responds to scrollLeft), AND confirmed by a human
 * actually using the table's scrollbar (trackpad/click-drag) in a real
 * running vault — no visual or usability defects found. Guides now render
 * correctly through every kind in the corpus, tables included.
 *
 * `.to-decor-marker` (Experiment 5a) needs the exact same override, for the
 * exact same reason: a widget atom's marker icon is a real DOM child
 * positioned outside its own box (`left: -gutter`, see decorations.ts), and
 * `contain: paint` clips real descendant elements, not just pseudo-element
 * backgrounds — a marker is present on EVERY widget atom (unlike a guide,
 * which needs an ancestor), so this can't stay gated on `.to-decor-guides`
 * alone, or a depth-0 table/callout/hr/html with no ancestor would have its
 * marker silently clipped.
 */
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > [contenteditable='false'].to-decor-guides,
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > [contenteditable='false'].to-decor-marker {
  position: relative;
  contain: none !important;
  overflow: visible !important;
}

.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-table-widget.to-decor-guides .table-wrapper,
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-table-widget.to-decor-marker .table-wrapper {
  overflow-x: auto !important;
  max-width: 100% !important;
}

/*
 * Fold-indicator/marker crowding (Experiment 5a follow-up): a heading's
 * native collapse chevron (`.cm-fold-indicator .collapse-indicator`) is
 * inserted at essentially the same "line start" position our own marker
 * icon anchors to, and its own `inset-inline-end: 0` (no extra offset)
 * lands it right on top of our marker at every heading level — confirmed
 * live via `document.styleSheets` inspection of the actual matching rule.
 *
 * A first attempt mirrored Obsidian's OWN list-line override for this same
 * problem (`padding-inline-end` + a matching negative `inset-inline-end`,
 * driven there by a `--list-bullet-end-padding` theme variable) — but
 * verified live (screenshot + rect measurement) that this widens the
 * chevron's own invisible hit-area rightward without actually relocating
 * the visible glyph — the two properties' effects on a shrink-to-fit
 * `position: absolute` flex box cancel out for the glyph's own position,
 * they don't move it. `transform: translateX()` instead moves the WHOLE
 * rendered box — glyph and hit-area together — unambiguously, confirmed
 * live to actually place the chevron to the left of our marker instead of
 * merely reducing the overlap.
 *
 * The shift is depth-independent by construction, the same way
 * `markerLeftShiftExpr` (decorations.ts) turned out to be: the chevron's
 * wrapper is inserted at essentially the same "text start" anchor our own
 * marker's target column is defined relative to, so the needed leftward
 * distance is always `gutter + half the marker's own width + a small
 * visual gap`, regardless of heading depth.
 *
 * A real spatial constraint, found via a real-vault report (a nested
 * heading, not this experiment's flat 2-level fixtures): for a heading at
 * depth N > 0, the guide column belonging to its shallowest ANCESTOR (not
 * its own depth) also passes through this line, one `--to-decor-unit`
 * closer to the marker than the shift above accounts for. Measured live at
 * depth 2: ~17px of clear space exists between that ancestor guide and the
 * marker's own left edge.
 *
 * A second real correction, also caught by the user (not assumed from the
 * box model): the WRAPPER box (`.collapse-indicator`, 22px, what the two
 * paragraphs above size against) is NOT the same as the visible glyph —
 * confirmed live via the actual `<svg>` element's own rect and `<path>`
 * `getBBox()`: the painted glyph is only ~10px wide, centered inside that
 * 22px box (~6px of invisible padding on each side, part of the click
 * hit-area but never painted). The 17px gap comfortably fits a 10px glyph;
 * the earlier "22px can't fit in 17px" conclusion measured the wrong box.
 * `deadSpace` below is that fixed ~6px (native Obsidian sizing, not one of
 * our own `rem`-scaled constants — kept as a bare px measured constant, the
 * same way `MARKER_GUTTER_REM` itself started as a measured value).
 */
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6.is-live-preview
  .cm-line.to-decor-block
  .cm-fold-indicator
  .collapse-indicator {
  transform: translateX(calc(-1 * (var(--to-marker-gutter, 1.25rem) + 0.425rem - 3px)));
}

/*
 * Marker icon (Experiment 5a): static styling for both delivery mechanisms
 * (decorations.ts's `MarkerWidget` for plain lines, `applyWidgetMarker` for
 * widget-replaced atoms) moved here per `eslint-plugin-obsidianmd`'s
 * `no-static-styles-assignment` rule — only the marker's own `left` offset
 * genuinely varies per instance (by depth/kind), so JS only ever sets that,
 * via `setCssProps({'--to-marker-left': ...})`.
 *
 * The two mechanisms can't share one positioning scheme (see decorations.ts's
 * own doc comments: plain lines rely on inline flow + `vertical-align:
 * baseline` to track the surrounding text's font metrics; a widget atom is
 * its own opaque, already-full-height box, stretched and flex-centered
 * instead) — `.to-decor-marker-icon` carries what's common to both,
 * `.to-decor-marker-icon--widget` overrides the rest for the widget-atom
 * case.
 */
.to-decor-marker-icon {
  display: inline-block;
  position: relative;
  left: var(--to-marker-left, 0px);
  vertical-align: baseline;
  width: 0.85rem; /* MARKER_ICON_REM, decorations.ts — keep in sync */
  height: 0.85rem;
  margin-left: -0.85rem; /* -1 * MARKER_ICON_REM */
  pointer-events: none;
  color: var(--text-faint);
}

.to-decor-marker-icon--widget {
  display: flex;
  position: absolute;
  top: 0;
  bottom: 0;
  height: auto;
  margin-left: 0;
  align-items: center;
  justify-content: center;
}

content.content:
1	## Context
2	
3	Outline mode's editing model (structural commands + keyboard grammar) is complete and
4	correct, but it was invisible: list items already render Obsidian's native bullet, so they
5	happen to look right, while heading and (especially) paragraph nodes render exactly like
6	stock markdown. The first dev-vault verification round hit this directly — flat,
7	paragraph-heavy documents gave no visual signal that outline mode was even on, let alone
8	where node boundaries fell.
9	
10	The design recorded here is not the one originally proposed. Three prior implementation
11	attempts failed in real vault use (cascade/`!important` fights, em/rem marker-size bugs,
12	native list hanging-indent corruption, fold-indicator collisions) — see
13	[docs/research/06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md](../../../docs/research/06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md).
14	A subsequent series of isolated, falsifiable experiments validated a different mechanism
15	per layer; this file records the architecture **as built and merged** (PRs #8, #10, #11),
16	not the original proposal. For the experiment-by-experiment rationale, the bugs found, and
17	the alternatives rejected along the way, see:
18	
19	- [docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md](../../../docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md) —
20	  series hub, ground rules, fixture corpus, final results table
21	- [docs/research/08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md](../../../docs/research/08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md) —
22	  additive-only indentation (kept)
23	- [docs/research/09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md](../../../docs/research/09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md) —
24	  guide lines, overlay (2a) vs. CSS stacked-gradient (2b, chosen)
25	- [docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md](../../../docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md) —
26	  block markers, SVG/DOM (5a, chosen) vs. CSS shapes (5b), head-to-head verdict, and the
27	  hardening checklist `tasks.md` section 3 draws from
28	- [docs/research/11-decoration-lessons.md](../../../docs/research/11-decoration-lessons.md) —
29	  cross-experiment findings (CSS cascade/box model, CM6 coordinate systems, Obsidian
30	  internals) worth reading before touching this code again
31	
32	The precedent this still follows is `src/plugin/grammar.ts` + `src/plugin/keymap.ts`: a
33	pure module (`decorate.ts`) that computes facts from `(OutlineDoc, position)` with no CM6
34	imports, and thin CM6 adapters (`decorations.ts`) that turn those facts into
35	decorations/DOM, gated by the same mode-source pattern already used for the keyboard
36	grammar and commands.
37	
38	## Goals / Non-Goals
39	
40	**Goals** (unchanged from the original proposal, all now met by the shipped design):
41	- Every node's tree depth is visible via an indentation guide, computed from its position
42	  in the parsed tree (not from raw markdown indentation/level), so heading subtrees, list
43	  nesting, and paragraph-adjacency children all read as one consistent hierarchy.
44	- Node kind is visible via a marker on nodes that have no adequate native leader of their
45	  own, without duplicating chrome nodes already have (list bullets).
46	- Decorations are strictly read-only: no document mutation, no cursor movement, no undo
47	  entries, ever.
48	- Same mode-gating guarantee as the rest of the plugin: off outline mode (or a file never
49	  toggled on), the editor is byte-for-byte stock Obsidian; toggling applies immediately, no
50	  reload.
51	- Mobile-safe (CM6 `Decoration`/CSS/DOM only, no Node/Electron APIs) and acceptable
52	  performance on typical single-note documents.
53	
54	**Non-Goals** (unchanged, still deferred):
55	- Fold chevrons or any fold state/persistence as a *decoration feature* — the marker layer
56	  does reposition Obsidian's *existing* native fold chevron via a CSS transform to avoid
57	  colliding with it (see D5), but introduces no new fold state, affordance, or persistence
58	  of its own.
59	- Zoom/breadcrumbs, drag handles, node-selection highlight — all depend on layers not built
60	  yet (selection model, zoom).
61	- Interactivity on the decorations themselves (click-to-select, click-to-fold, hover
62	  affordances) — passive chrome, not a new input surface. (The marker mechanism's use of a
63	  real DOM widget makes this easier to add later than the original line-attribute design
64	  would have — see D2 — but nothing here adds it.)
65	- Configurable marker glyph/guide styling via a settings UI, beyond the one shipped
66	  `markerVisibility` axis (D5) — user CSS snippets remain the escape hatch for anything
67	  finer-grained.
68	- Reading (preview) view — untouched by construction; this plugin only registers CM6 editor
69	  extensions, never a `MarkdownPostProcessor`.
70	
71	## Architecture: three layers, one shared pure module
72	
73	`decorate.ts` (no CM6/Obsidian imports, unit-testable directly against `parse()` output)
74	computes two kinds of per-line fact from an `OutlineDoc`, both walking the tree in document
75	order:
76	
77	- `decorate(doc): LineDecorationFact[]` — `{ lineNumber, depth, isFirstLine,
78	  hasNativeMarker, isAtom, isListItem, supplementalDepth, kind, hasChildren }` per line.
79	  `supplementalDepth` is the additive-indentation quantity (below); `kind`/`hasChildren`
80	  feed the marker layer.
81	- `computeLineGuides(doc): LineGuideFact[]` — `{ lineNumber, guideDepths, isGapLine }` per
82	  line, a strict superset of `decorate()`'s line coverage (it also covers blank
83	  `trailingGap` separator lines, which carry no `decorate()` fact at all).
84	
85	`decorations.ts` turns these into three CM6 `ViewPlugin`s, all gated by a shared
86	`DecorationSource` (`ModeSource` + the current `markerVisibility` setting) and all checking
87	`isNestedEditor(view)` before computing anything (see "Nested-editor gating" below):
88	
89	1. **`DecorationsPlugin`** — line decorations for indentation + guides (additive
90	   indentation, D3/D4 below; guides, D1).
91	2. **`MarkersPlugin`** — a *separate* `ViewPlugin` producing `Decoration.widget`s for the
92	   block-marker icon on plain lines (D5). Kept separate from (1) rather than merged into
93	   one `RangeSetBuilder`, specifically so CM6's own decoration-merging (multiple sources at
94	   the same position) does the work instead of this code having to reason about
95	   `Decoration.line`/`Decoration.widget` ordering itself.
96	3. **`MarginCompensation`** — a `ViewPlugin` that runs after render (`docViewUpdate`) and
97	   directly patches DOM for two things CM6 decorations cannot reach: widget-replaced atom
98	   elements (table/callout/html/hr — D2), and Obsidian's native "readable line width"
99	   base margin, which every `.cm-line` gets regardless of our own decorations and which our
100	   own `margin-left`/`padding-left` values must add to, not replace.
101	
102	### Layer 1 — additive-only indentation
103	
104	`padding-left` = `depth × unit` for block lines (heading/paragraph) and atom lines
105	(code/table/callout/quote/html/hr) — additive by construction, since there is no native
106	`padding-left`/`margin-left` on these kinds to conflict with. Atoms use `margin-left`
107	instead of `padding-left` for the same value: padding shifts an element's *content*, not
108	its own border/background box, which is invisible for plain text but wrong for an atom that
109	renders a visible box.
110	
111	List items are handled differently and never have their native `text-indent`/`padding-left`
112	hang pair touched at all. Instead, `supplementalDepth` (the count of non-list-item
113	ancestors above the nearest list root) is applied as `margin-left` — a box-model property
114	native list rendering doesn't otherwise use — on top of native rendering. A list with no
115	non-list-item ancestors has `supplementalDepth` 0 everywhere in it, which makes it
116	byte-identical to outline-mode-off. This is a permanent regression invariant covered by
117	dedicated tests, not an incidental property (see spec.md).
118	
119	Widget-replaced atoms (table/callout/html/hr — Obsidian renders these as opaque
120	`.cm-embed-block`/`.hr` replacement elements in Live Preview) get zero effect from any CM6
121	`Decoration.line` — confirmed live, not assumed. `MarginCompensation` patches their
122	`margin-left` directly via an inline `!important` style after each render, additionally
123	compensating for native padding it reads live via `getComputedStyle` (never hardcoded, so
124	it stays correct across themes) and clamped at zero so a depth-0 atom never goes negative.
125	
126	Full history, the three real-vault bugs found and fixed, and the deferred (not fixed) list
127	hang-width cosmetic gap:
128	[docs/research/08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md](../../../docs/research/08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md).
129	
130	### Layer 2 — indentation guides (CSS stacked-gradient)
131	
132	One `--to-guides` CSS custom property per line — a comma-joined list of
133	`repeating-linear-gradient(...)` layers, one per active ancestor depth — consumed by a
134	single shared `::after` pseudo-element (the `@replit/codemirror-indentation-markers`
135	technique). O(1) DOM nodes per line regardless of depth; no pixel measurement.
136	
137	A depth is "active" on a line when a strict, non-list-item ancestor at that depth owns a
138	guide. List-item ancestors never own a guide: Obsidian's native "Show indent guides"
139	already connects one bullet precisely to the next within a list, and a second, fixed-unit
140	mechanism running alongside it either doubles the line or reads as unevenly spaced against
141	native per-level width. A non-list ancestor bridging into a list still gets its own guide,
142	reaching all the way through the list's lines.
143	
144	Margin-shifted lines (atoms, list items) widen their own `::after` box leftward by
145	`--to-own-shift` (exactly their own known `margin-left` contribution) to reach a shallower
146	ancestor's column — a `::before`/`::after` pseudo-element's box does not have to match its
147	containing block's dimensions, and nothing in the ancestor chain up to `.cm-scroller` clips
148	that overflow. Widget-replaced atoms need one additional override:
149	`contain: paint !important`, which Obsidian's own `app.css` sets on all four widget kinds
150	and which clips descendant painting independently of `overflow`. The override is scoped to
151	match Obsidian's own selector specificity (not `!important`-escalation, which has nowhere
152	further to go once both sides already use it). The table widget needed one more: its own
153	`overflow-x: auto` (for horizontal scroll of wide tables) lives on the *same* element the
154	`contain` override targets; moving `overflow-x: auto` onto Obsidian's own inner
155	`.table-wrapper` element decouples "let the guide bleed past this box's edge" from "keep
156	wide content scrolling," so both behaviors hold simultaneously.
157	
158	Blank `trailingGap` lines also carry a guide fact (a leaf's own gap uses its own
159	`guideDepths`; a node-with-children's gap uses `childGuideDepths`, since that gap already
160	sits inside the node's own subtree) — guides render continuously through blank separator
161	lines, not just through node content.
162	
163	Full history including the "guides can't reach past a shifted box" false claim and its
164	correction, the blockquote `::before` collision, the readable-line-width interaction, and
165	the table fix: [docs/research/09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md](../../../docs/research/09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md).
166	
167	### Layer 3 — block markers (SVG icons, DOM mechanism)
168	
169	A small, fixed-size (`rem`, never `em` — the historical marker-size bug class, `font-size`
170	resolving against the wrong line's context), self-drawn SVG icon per node kind, distinct
171	per kind (heading/paragraph/code/table/callout/quote/html/hr), built via DOM APIs
172	(`createElementNS`), never a data-URI. List items get no marker — the native bullet/number
173	already does that job (same exclusion guides use).
174	
175	Two delivery mechanisms, split the same way indentation is:
176	
177	- **Plain lines** (heading/paragraph/code/quote): a CM6 `Decoration.widget`
178	  (`side: -1`) inserted at the line's first character, kept in normal inline flow
179	  (`display: inline-block`, `vertical-align: baseline`) rather than absolutely positioned —
180	  this gets correct vertical alignment "for free" from the browser's own text layout,
181	  scaling naturally with heading font size, instead of needing a live-measured Y-position
182	  (the alternative CSS-shape design, 5b, needed exactly this and it was the more expensive
183	  path — see the head-to-head verdict in doc 10).
184	- **Widget-replaced atoms** (table/callout/html/hr): a real DOM child injected directly by
185	  `MarginCompensation`, the same escape hatch already used for their `margin-left` — CM6
186	  decorations have zero effect on these elements.
187	
188	Both mechanisms place the icon horizontally centered on the shared guide-line column (not
189	flush with its left edge), so a guide passes through a marker's visual center exactly the
190	way a native nested list's connecting line passes through its own bullet — a geometry both
191	5a and 5b independently converged on. Placing every non-list line's marker requires
192	reserving a small `--to-marker-gutter` additively on top of the existing depth formula, even
193	at depth 0 — flat/top-level nodes have no other gutter to draw into. Never appending a
194	marker child into a plain `.cm-line` is a hard invariant, not a style choice: an earlier
195	version tried it (to get live-measured height for code fences) and pegged Obsidian's
196	renderer at 100%+ CPU, almost certainly CM6's own DOM-mutation observer reacting to the
197	unexpected child in a feedback loop; plain-line markers stay on the CM6-native
198	`Decoration.widget` path for this reason.
199	
200	**Marker visibility.** `markerVisibility` (`'all'` / `'with-children'` /
201	`'headings-and-paragraphs'`) is a real, persisted, user-facing setting
202	(`mode-registry.ts`), not a temporary toggle — added after review found that a marker reads
203	well as a "crown on top of the guide line" for a branch node's kind, but adds little for a
204	leaf (most leaf atom kinds already carry their own native visual style: a code fence's
205	background, a callout's colored bar). `'with-children'` hides markers on any leaf, atoms
206	included, via a new `hasChildren` field on `LineDecorationFact`. `'headings-and-paragraphs'`
207	keys off kind, not per-instance state: those are the only two marker-eligible kinds that
208	can ever have children in this tree model. The gutter reservation stays unconditional
209	regardless of this setting — hiding a marker never reflows text; only whether the icon
210	itself is drawn changes.
211	
212	Full history, including the fold-chevron collision fix, the three placement variants
213	explored, and the 5a-vs-5b comparison table:
214	[docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md](../../../docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md).
215	
216	### Nested-editor gating
217	
218	Obsidian renders an actively-edited table cell in Live Preview as its own separate,
219	independent CM6 `EditorView`, mounted inside the outer table widget's own DOM
220	(`.cm-embed-block.cm-table-widget` → … → a nested `.cm-editor`).
221	`registerEditorExtension` (`main.ts`) applies this whole extension to *every* CM6 instance
222	Obsidian creates app-wide, this nested one included — and its "document" is just the cell's
223	raw text, which `parse()`/`decorate()` classify as a plain paragraph, picking up markers and
224	depth-based indentation exactly like real top-level content, visibly corrupting the cell
225	being edited. `editorInfoField` (the mode-source gate used everywhere else in this plugin)
226	resolves to the exact same outer `MarkdownView` for both the nested and the real editor, so
227	state alone cannot distinguish them — only DOM ancestry can:
228	`view.dom.closest('.cm-embed-block') !== null`. This is why all three decoration mechanisms
229	are `ViewPlugin`s (which have `view`/DOM access) rather than plain `StateField`s (which do
230	not) — `isNestedEditor(view)` is checked by each before computing anything, returning
231	`Decoration.none` / doing nothing for a nested instance. Independently confirmed in the wild
232	(obsidian-lapel hits the identical leak in its own gutter markers, fixed there only as a CSS
233	hide rather than stopping computation). Full account:
234	[docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md](../../../docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md#follow-up-round-4-a-genuine-architectural-bug-found-via-a-flaky-test--decorations-leaking-into-obsidians-own-nested-per-cell-editors).
235	
236	## Decisions
237	
238	Renumbered from the original proposal's D1–D6 to reflect what actually shipped; the
239	original numbering and its "void" annotations are preserved in git history
240	(see the pre-experiment version of this file) rather than repeated here.
241	
242	### D1. Guides are line-attribute decorations (CSS custom property + shared `::after`), not widgets
243	Confirmed as originally proposed for the *guide* layer specifically: `Decoration.line` sets
244	a `--to-guides` custom property, consumed by a single stylesheet `::after` rule — no widget,
245	no extra DOM node per guide. This part of the original D1/D2 pairing survived the
246	postmortem's void largely intact once combined with the `--to-own-shift`
247	box-widening technique (Layer 2 above) that the original design didn't have. Depth itself
248	(`--to-depth`) is likewise still a plain CSS custom property, consumed by a `calc()` rule —
249	the original per-depth-class alternative remains rejected for the reason the original
250	proposal gave (unbounded nesting depth).
251	
252	### D2. The block marker is a real DOM element (`Decoration.widget` / direct DOM injection), not a line attribute
253	This reverses the original D2. The original design(rejected by the postmortem) drew the
254	marker as a `::before`/background rule, reasoning that a non-interactive marker didn't need
255	widget DOM-lifecycle cost. Experiment 5's head-to-head (5a icons via a real widget vs. 5b a
256	uniform dot painted on the same `::after` guides use) found the opposite: vertical
257	centering on "the first rendered visual row" is fundamentally a rendering fact no CSS-only
258	formula can derive, so the line-attribute variant (5b) ended up needing a genuinely new
259	live-pixel-measurement subsystem anyway to get it right, plus ongoing coordinate-coupling
260	bugs from sharing one pseudo-element's coordinate space with the guide gradient. The
261	DOM-element variant (5a) gets correct vertical alignment for free from the browser's own
262	inline-flow text layout, at full opacity (not sharing the guide's `0.6 opacity`), and stays
263	open to future interactivity if a later change ever wants it. Full comparison:
264	doc 10's "Head-to-head: comparison and verdict."
265	
266	### D3. Depth is derived by walking the parsed tree, not from source indentation/level
267	Unchanged from the original proposal and confirmed as built: a node's depth is its distance
268	from the document root in the `OutlineDoc` tree, computed by walking `doc.children`
269	recursively in `decorate()`/`computeLineGuides()` — the same walk shape as `startLine` in
270	`grammar.ts`. This is what makes heading level, list indentation, and paragraph-adjacency
271	depth collapse into one visual language regardless of how many `#` characters or spaces are
272	in the source.
273	
274	### D4. Full-document recompute on every relevant transaction, no incremental diffing
275	Confirmed as built, now across three `ViewPlugin`s rather than the originally-proposed
276	single `ViewPlugin`: `DecorationsPlugin`, `MarkersPlugin`, and `MarginCompensation` each
277	independently call `parse(view.state.doc.toString())` on every update — the 2b baseline
278	already did this twice before Experiment 5 added a third pass. Same asymptotic budget
279	already accepted for the keyboard grammar and structural commands, tripled constant factor;
280	fine for normal notes, flagged as a hardening item (consolidate to one shared `parse()`/
281	`decorate()` pass per transaction) rather than a blocker — see `tasks.md`.
282	
283	### D5. Marker scope: every non-list-item kind, gated by a persisted visibility setting
284	This revises the original D5 ("paragraphs only"). Real-content review during Experiment 5
285	found that per-kind icons read as legible and useful well beyond paragraphs — headings,
286	code, tables, callouts, quotes, html, and hr all get a distinct icon, gated only by
287	`markerVisibility` (`'all'` / `'with-children'` / `'headings-and-paragraphs'`, default
288	`'all'`), not by kind. List items remain permanently excluded (native bullet/number already
289	serves the purpose the original D5 was protecting). The original rationale — don't double
290	up on chrome that already reads clearly — is now served by the visibility setting rather
291	than a fixed kind restriction, since real usage showed the "which kinds need a marker"
292	question is closer to a matter of taste than a fact fixed at design time.
293	
294	### D6. Gating reuses (and extends) the `ModeSource` contract
295	The decoration `ViewPlugin`s take a `DecorationSource` — `ModeSource` (`isOutline(path)`,
296	unchanged from the original proposal, same contract the keyboard grammar depends on) plus
297	`markerVisibility`, read fresh on every recompute so a live setting change takes effect on
298	the next transaction without a rebuild. When the file isn't in outline mode, or the view is
299	a nested per-cell editor (see "Nested-editor gating" above — a refinement the original
300	design didn't anticipate), every plugin's `decorations` field is the empty `DecorationSet`.
301	
302	## Risks / Trade-offs
303	
304	- **Triple full-reparse per transaction** (D4) — same accepted budget as before, now with a
305	  3x constant. Not yet a problem on normal notes; consolidation is a tracked hardening task,
306	  not a blocker.
307	- **Two hardcoded fold-chevron measurement constants** in the marker-repositioning CSS
308	  (`decorations.ts`) are the one place this design violates its own "read native values
309	  live" rule — a theme or Obsidian update that resizes the chevron degrades layout
310	  cosmetically. 5b's session solved the equivalent problem with live measurement; porting
311	  that approach is a tracked hardening task.
312	- **CSS guide alignment across themes/fonts** — mitigated in practice (verified across
313	  bundled light/dark themes and one community theme), but RTL text, IME composition, and a
314	  wider community-theme sweep remain untested; tracked as hardening/polish.
315	- **Community CSS snippets/themes overriding our styling** — narrowly scoped `to-` prefixed
316	  class names, `!important` only where matching Obsidian's own specificity requires it, not
317	  as a first resort; documented as a known limitation rather than an unbounded specificity
318	  war.
319	- **Two documented invariants must survive future refactors**: (a) DOM injection into
320	  widget-atom subtrees relies on Obsidian never re-diffing those opaque subtrees internally
321	  — an undocumented-to-Obsidian invariant whose failure mode would be re-injection
322	  flicker/duplicated markers; (b) never append a child into a plain `.cm-line` — confirmed
323	  to peg the renderer via CM6's mutation-observer feedback loop. Both are called out
324	  explicitly here and in `tasks.md` so a future change doesn't rediscover them the hard way.
325	- **Visual redundancy if obsidian-outliner/obsidian-zoom are also enabled** — unchanged from
326	  the original proposal, already covered by the existing one-time coexistence warning
327	  Notice.
328	
329	## Migration Plan
330	
331	Unchanged from the original proposal: purely additive UI layer, no data model, no file
332	content, no plugin-data schema changes beyond the new `markerVisibility` field (which has a
333	default and degrades gracefully if absent from persisted data). Ships as a normal minor
334	change: build, unit tests (`tests/decorate.test.ts`), e2e DOM/screenshot coverage
335	(`e2e/specs/50-decorations.e2e.ts`, `51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts`,
336	`52-block-markers-icons.e2e.ts`), manual dev-vault visual pass. No rollback concerns beyond
337	disabling the plugin, which already restores stock rendering.
338	
339	## Open Questions
340	
341	- **Shrinking only our own added list margin** — raised during Experiment 1's review, not
342	  yet decided. List items sit visibly further right than a same-depth sibling
343	  paragraph/blockquote, traced to Obsidian's own native list hang (`text-indent`/
344	  `padding-left`) pre-existing in vanilla Obsidian, not something this design introduces —
345	  but a narrower, still-additive-only fix (reduce only our own added margin by the list
346	  root's own native hang width, read live) stays open as a possible follow-up. Two risks
347	  noted but unresolved: clamping so a shallow `supplementalDepth` never goes negative, and
348	  compensating from the list *root*'s own hang, not each item's (nested items can have
349	  wider markers with different native hang widths). See doc 10's "Open question" section.
350	- **Marker/guide interactivity** (click-to-select, click-to-fold, per-heading-level markers)
351	  remains explicitly deferred, per the Non-Goals above. Doc 10's prior-art addendum
352	  (obsidian-lapel) records a concrete idea for how a future change might attach this to the
353	  real DOM widget the marker layer already has, without committing to it here.
354

file.content:
## Context

Outline mode's editing model (structural commands + keyboard grammar) is complete and
correct, but it was invisible: list items already render Obsidian's native bullet, so they
happen to look right, while heading and (especially) paragraph nodes render exactly like
stock markdown. The first dev-vault verification round hit this directly — flat,
paragraph-heavy documents gave no visual signal that outline mode was even on, let alone
where node boundaries fell.

The design recorded here is not the one originally proposed. Three prior implementation
attempts failed in real vault use (cascade/`!important` fights, em/rem marker-size bugs,
native list hanging-indent corruption, fold-indicator collisions) — see
[docs/research/06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md](../../../docs/research/06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md).
A subsequent series of isolated, falsifiable experiments validated a different mechanism
per layer; this file records the architecture **as built and merged** (PRs #8, #10, #11),
not the original proposal. For the experiment-by-experiment rationale, the bugs found, and
the alternatives rejected along the way, see:

- [docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md](../../../docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md) —
  series hub, ground rules, fixture corpus, final results table
- [docs/research/08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md](../../../docs/research/08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md) —
  additive-only indentation (kept)
- [docs/research/09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md](../../../docs/research/09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md) —
  guide lines, overlay (2a) vs. CSS stacked-gradient (2b, chosen)
- [docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md](../../../docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md) —
  block markers, SVG/DOM (5a, chosen) vs. CSS shapes (5b), head-to-head verdict, and the
  hardening checklist `tasks.md` section 3 draws from
- [docs/research/11-decoration-lessons.md](../../../docs/research/11-decoration-lessons.md) —
  cross-experiment findings (CSS cascade/box model, CM6 coordinate systems, Obsidian
  internals) worth reading before touching this code again

The precedent this still follows is `src/plugin/grammar.ts` + `src/plugin/keymap.ts`: a
pure module (`decorate.ts`) that computes facts from `(OutlineDoc, position)` with no CM6
imports, and thin CM6 adapters (`decorations.ts`) that turn those facts into
decorations/DOM, gated by the same mode-source pattern already used for the keyboard
grammar and commands.

## Goals / Non-Goals

**Goals** (unchanged from the original proposal, all now met by the shipped design):
- Every node's tree depth is visible via an indentation guide, computed from its position
  in the parsed tree (not from raw markdown indentation/level), so heading subtrees, list
  nesting, and paragraph-adjacency children all read as one consistent hierarchy.
- Node kind is visible via a marker on nodes that have no adequate native leader of their
  own, without duplicating chrome nodes already have (list bullets).
- Decorations are strictly read-only: no document mutation, no cursor movement, no undo
  entries, ever.
- Same mode-gating guarantee as the rest of the plugin: off outline mode (or a file never
  toggled on), the editor is byte-for-byte stock Obsidian; toggling applies immediately, no
  reload.
- Mobile-safe (CM6 `Decoration`/CSS/DOM only, no Node/Electron APIs) and acceptable
  performance on typical single-note documents.

**Non-Goals** (unchanged, still deferred):
- Fold chevrons or any fold state/persistence as a *decoration feature* — the marker layer
  does reposition Obsidian's *existing* native fold chevron via a CSS transform to avoid
  colliding with it (see D5), but introduces no new fold state, affordance, or persistence
  of its own.
- Zoom/breadcrumbs, drag handles, node-selection highlight — all depend on layers not built
  yet (selection model, zoom).
- Interactivity on the decorations themselves (click-to-select, click-to-fold, hover
  affordances) — passive chrome, not a new input surface. (The marker mechanism's use of a
  real DOM widget makes this easier to add later than the original line-attribute design
  would have — see D2 — but nothing here adds it.)
- Configurable marker glyph/guide styling via a settings UI, beyond the one shipped
  `markerVisibility` axis (D5) — user CSS snippets remain the escape hatch for anything
  finer-grained.
- Reading (preview) view — untouched by construction; this plugin only registers CM6 editor
  extensions, never a `MarkdownPostProcessor`.

## Architecture: three layers, one shared pure module

`decorate.ts` (no CM6/Obsidian imports, unit-testable directly against `parse()` output)
computes two kinds of per-line fact from an `OutlineDoc`, both walking the tree in document
order:

- `decorate(doc): LineDecorationFact[]` — `{ lineNumber, depth, isFirstLine,
  hasNativeMarker, isAtom, isListItem, supplementalDepth, kind, hasChildren }` per line.
  `supplementalDepth` is the additive-indentation quantity (below); `kind`/`hasChildren`
  feed the marker layer.
- `computeLineGuides(doc): LineGuideFact[]` — `{ lineNumber, guideDepths, isGapLine }` per
  line, a strict superset of `decorate()`'s line coverage (it also covers blank
  `trailingGap` separator lines, which carry no `decorate()` fact at all).

`decorations.ts` turns these into three CM6 `ViewPlugin`s, all gated by a shared
`DecorationSource` (`ModeSource` + the current `markerVisibility` setting) and all checking
`isNestedEditor(view)` before computing anything (see "Nested-editor gating" below):

1. **`DecorationsPlugin`** — line decorations for indentation + guides (additive
   indentation, D3/D4 below; guides, D1).
2. **`MarkersPlugin`** — a *separate* `ViewPlugin` producing `Decoration.widget`s for the
   block-marker icon on plain lines (D5). Kept separate from (1) rather than merged into
   one `RangeSetBuilder`, specifically so CM6's own decoration-merging (multiple sources at
   the same position) does the work instead of this code having to reason about
   `Decoration.line`/`Decoration.widget` ordering itself.
3. **`MarginCompensation`** — a `ViewPlugin` that runs after render (`docViewUpdate`) and
   directly patches DOM for two things CM6 decorations cannot reach: widget-replaced atom
   elements (table/callout/html/hr — D2), and Obsidian's native "readable line width"
   base margin, which every `.cm-line` gets regardless of our own decorations and which our
   own `margin-left`/`padding-left` values must add to, not replace.

### Layer 1 — additive-only indentation

`padding-left` = `depth × unit` for block lines (heading/paragraph) and atom lines
(code/table/callout/quote/html/hr) — additive by construction, since there is no native
`padding-left`/`margin-left` on these kinds to conflict with. Atoms use `margin-left`
instead of `padding-left` for the same value: padding shifts an element's *content*, not
its own border/background box, which is invisible for plain text but wrong for an atom that
renders a visible box.

List items are handled differently and never have their native `text-indent`/`padding-left`
hang pair touched at all. Instead, `supplementalDepth` (the count of non-list-item
ancestors above the nearest list root) is applied as `margin-left` — a box-model property
native list rendering doesn't otherwise use — on top of native rendering. A list with no
non-list-item ancestors has `supplementalDepth` 0 everywhere in it, which makes it
byte-identical to outline-mode-off. This is a permanent regression invariant covered by
dedicated tests, not an incidental property (see spec.md).

Widget-replaced atoms (table/callout/html/hr — Obsidian renders these as opaque
`.cm-embed-block`/`.hr` replacement elements in Live Preview) get zero effect from any CM6
`Decoration.line` — confirmed live, not assumed. `MarginCompensation` patches their
`margin-left` directly via an inline `!important` style after each render, additionally
compensating for native padding it reads live via `getComputedStyle` (never hardcoded, so
it stays correct across themes) and clamped at zero so a depth-0 atom never goes negative.

Full history, the three real-vault bugs found and fixed, and the deferred (not fixed) list
hang-width cosmetic gap:
[docs/research/08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md](../../../docs/research/08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md).

### Layer 2 — indentation guides (CSS stacked-gradient)

One `--to-guides` CSS custom property per line — a comma-joined list of
`repeating-linear-gradient(...)` layers, one per active ancestor depth — consumed by a
single shared `::after` pseudo-element (the `@replit/codemirror-indentation-markers`
technique). O(1) DOM nodes per line regardless of depth; no pixel measurement.

A depth is "active" on a line when a strict, non-list-item ancestor at that depth owns a
guide. List-item ancestors never own a guide: Obsidian's native "Show indent guides"
already connects one bullet precisely to the next within a list, and a second, fixed-unit
mechanism running alongside it either doubles the line or reads as unevenly spaced against
native per-level width. A non-list ancestor bridging into a list still gets its own guide,
reaching all the way through the list's lines.

Margin-shifted lines (atoms, list items) widen their own `::after` box leftward by
`--to-own-shift` (exactly their own known `margin-left` contribution) to reach a shallower
ancestor's column — a `::before`/`::after` pseudo-element's box does not have to match its
containing block's dimensions, and nothing in the ancestor chain up to `.cm-scroller` clips
that overflow. Widget-replaced atoms need one additional override:
`contain: paint !important`, which Obsidian's own `app.css` sets on all four widget kinds
and which clips descendant painting independently of `overflow`. The override is scoped to
match Obsidian's own selector specificity (not `!important`-escalation, which has nowhere
further to go once both sides already use it). The table widget needed one more: its own
`overflow-x: auto` (for horizontal scroll of wide tables) lives on the *same* element the
`contain` override targets; moving `overflow-x: auto` onto Obsidian's own inner
`.table-wrapper` element decouples "let the guide bleed past this box's edge" from "keep
wide content scrolling," so both behaviors hold simultaneously.

Blank `trailingGap` lines also carry a guide fact (a leaf's own gap uses its own
`guideDepths`; a node-with-children's gap uses `childGuideDepths`, since that gap already
sits inside the node's own subtree) — guides render continuously through blank separator
lines, not just through node content.

Full history including the "guides can't reach past a shifted box" false claim and its
correction, the blockquote `::before` collision, the readable-line-width interaction, and
the table fix: [docs/research/09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md](../../../docs/research/09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md).

### Layer 3 — block markers (SVG icons, DOM mechanism)

A small, fixed-size (`rem`, never `em` — the historical marker-size bug class, `font-size`
resolving against the wrong line's context), self-drawn SVG icon per node kind, distinct
per kind (heading/paragraph/code/table/callout/quote/html/hr), built via DOM APIs
(`createElementNS`), never a data-URI. List items get no marker — the native bullet/number
already does that job (same exclusion guides use).

Two delivery mechanisms, split the same way indentation is:

- **Plain lines** (heading/paragraph/code/quote): a CM6 `Decoration.widget`
  (`side: -1`) inserted at the line's first character, kept in normal inline flow
  (`display: inline-block`, `vertical-align: baseline`) rather than absolutely positioned —
  this gets correct vertical alignment "for free" from the browser's own text layout,
  scaling naturally with heading font size, instead of needing a live-measured Y-position
  (the alternative CSS-shape design, 5b, needed exactly this and it was the more expensive
  path — see the head-to-head verdict in doc 10).
- **Widget-replaced atoms** (table/callout/html/hr): a real DOM child injected directly by
  `MarginCompensation`, the same escape hatch already used for their `margin-left` — CM6
  decorations have zero effect on these elements.

Both mechanisms place the icon horizontally centered on the shared guide-line column (not
flush with its left edge), so a guide passes through a marker's visual center exactly the
way a native nested list's connecting line passes through its own bullet — a geometry both
5a and 5b independently converged on. Placing every non-list line's marker requires
reserving a small `--to-marker-gutter` additively on top of the existing depth formula, even
at depth 0 — flat/top-level nodes have no other gutter to draw into. Never appending a
marker child into a plain `.cm-line` is a hard invariant, not a style choice: an earlier
version tried it (to get live-measured height for code fences) and pegged Obsidian's
renderer at 100%+ CPU, almost certainly CM6's own DOM-mutation observer reacting to the
unexpected child in a feedback loop; plain-line markers stay on the CM6-native
`Decoration.widget` path for this reason.

**Marker visibility.** `markerVisibility` (`'all'` / `'with-children'` /
`'headings-and-paragraphs'`) is a real, persisted, user-facing setting
(`mode-registry.ts`), not a temporary toggle — added after review found that a marker reads
well as a "crown on top of the guide line" for a branch node's kind, but adds little for a
leaf (most leaf atom kinds already carry their own native visual style: a code fence's
background, a callout's colored bar). `'with-children'` hides markers on any leaf, atoms
included, via a new `hasChildren` field on `LineDecorationFact`. `'headings-and-paragraphs'`
keys off kind, not per-instance state: those are the only two marker-eligible kinds that
can ever have children in this tree model. The gutter reservation stays unconditional
regardless of this setting — hiding a marker never reflows text; only whether the icon
itself is drawn changes.

Full history, including the fold-chevron collision fix, the three placement variants
explored, and the 5a-vs-5b comparison table:
[docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md](../../../docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md).

### Nested-editor gating

Obsidian renders an actively-edited table cell in Live Preview as its own separate,
independent CM6 `EditorView`, mounted inside the outer table widget's own DOM
(`.cm-embed-block.cm-table-widget` → … → a nested `.cm-editor`).
`registerEditorExtension` (`main.ts`) applies this whole extension to *every* CM6 instance
Obsidian creates app-wide, this nested one included — and its "document" is just the cell's
raw text, which `parse()`/`decorate()` classify as a plain paragraph, picking up markers and
depth-based indentation exactly like real top-level content, visibly corrupting the cell
being edited. `editorInfoField` (the mode-source gate used everywhere else in this plugin)
resolves to the exact same outer `MarkdownView` for both the nested and the real editor, so
state alone cannot distinguish them — only DOM ancestry can:
`view.dom.closest('.cm-embed-block') !== null`. This is why all three decoration mechanisms
are `ViewPlugin`s (which have `view`/DOM access) rather than plain `StateField`s (which do
not) — `isNestedEditor(view)` is checked by each before computing anything, returning
`Decoration.none` / doing nothing for a nested instance. Independently confirmed in the wild
(obsidian-lapel hits the identical leak in its own gutter markers, fixed there only as a CSS
hide rather than stopping computation). Full account:
[docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md](../../../docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md#follow-up-round-4-a-genuine-architectural-bug-found-via-a-flaky-test--decorations-leaking-into-obsidians-own-nested-per-cell-editors).

## Decisions

Renumbered from the original proposal's D1–D6 to reflect what actually shipped; the
original numbering and its "void" annotations are preserved in git history
(see the pre-experiment version of this file) rather than repeated here.

### D1. Guides are line-attribute decorations (CSS custom property + shared `::after`), not widgets
Confirmed as originally proposed for the *guide* layer specifically: `Decoration.line` sets
a `--to-guides` custom property, consumed by a single stylesheet `::after` rule — no widget,
no extra DOM node per guide. This part of the original D1/D2 pairing survived the
postmortem's void largely intact once combined with the `--to-own-shift`
box-widening technique (Layer 2 above) that the original design didn't have. Depth itself
(`--to-depth`) is likewise still a plain CSS custom property, consumed by a `calc()` rule —
the original per-depth-class alternative remains rejected for the reason the original
proposal gave (unbounded nesting depth).

### D2. The block marker is a real DOM element (`Decoration.widget` / direct DOM injection), not a line attribute
This reverses the original D2. The original design(rejected by the postmortem) drew the
marker as a `::before`/background rule, reasoning that a non-interactive marker didn't need
widget DOM-lifecycle cost. Experiment 5's head-to-head (5a icons via a real widget vs. 5b a
uniform dot painted on the same `::after` guides use) found the opposite: vertical
centering on "the first rendered visual row" is fundamentally a rendering fact no CSS-only
formula can derive, so the line-attribute variant (5b) ended up needing a genuinely new
live-pixel-measurement subsystem anyway to get it right, plus ongoing coordinate-coupling
bugs from sharing one pseudo-element's coordinate space with the guide gradient. The
DOM-element variant (5a) gets correct vertical alignment for free from the browser's own
inline-flow text layout, at full opacity (not sharing the guide's `0.6 opacity`), and stays
open to future interactivity if a later change ever wants it. Full comparison:
doc 10's "Head-to-head: comparison and verdict."

### D3. Depth is derived by walking the parsed tree, not from source indentation/level
Unchanged from the original proposal and confirmed as built: a node's depth is its distance
from the document root in the `OutlineDoc` tree, computed by walking `doc.children`
recursively in `decorate()`/`computeLineGuides()` — the same walk shape as `startLine` in
`grammar.ts`. This is what makes heading level, list indentation, and paragraph-adjacency
depth collapse into one visual language regardless of how many `#` characters or spaces are
in the source.

### D4. Full-document recompute on every relevant transaction, no incremental diffing
Confirmed as built, now across three `ViewPlugin`s rather than the originally-proposed
single `ViewPlugin`: `DecorationsPlugin`, `MarkersPlugin`, and `MarginCompensation` each
independently call `parse(view.state.doc.toString())` on every update — the 2b baseline
already did this twice before Experiment 5 added a third pass. Same asymptotic budget
already accepted for the keyboard grammar and structural commands, tripled constant factor;
fine for normal notes, flagged as a hardening item (consolidate to one shared `parse()`/
`decorate()` pass per transaction) rather than a blocker — see `tasks.md`.

### D5. Marker scope: every non-list-item kind, gated by a persisted visibility setting
This revises the original D5 ("paragraphs only"). Real-content review during Experiment 5
found that per-kind icons read as legible and useful well beyond paragraphs — headings,
code, tables, callouts, quotes, html, and hr all get a distinct icon, gated only by
`markerVisibility` (`'all'` / `'with-children'` / `'headings-and-paragraphs'`, default
`'all'`), not by kind. List items remain permanently excluded (native bullet/number already
serves the purpose the original D5 was protecting). The original rationale — don't double
up on chrome that already reads clearly — is now served by the visibility setting rather
than a fixed kind restriction, since real usage showed the "which kinds need a marker"
question is closer to a matter of taste than a fact fixed at design time.

### D6. Gating reuses (and extends) the `ModeSource` contract
The decoration `ViewPlugin`s take a `DecorationSource` — `ModeSource` (`isOutline(path)`,
unchanged from the original proposal, same contract the keyboard grammar depends on) plus
`markerVisibility`, read fresh on every recompute so a live setting change takes effect on
the next transaction without a rebuild. When the file isn't in outline mode, or the view is
a nested per-cell editor (see "Nested-editor gating" above — a refinement the original
design didn't anticipate), every plugin's `decorations` field is the empty `DecorationSet`.

## Risks / Trade-offs

- **Triple full-reparse per transaction** (D4) — same accepted budget as before, now with a
  3x constant. Not yet a problem on normal notes; consolidation is a tracked hardening task,
  not a blocker.
- **Two hardcoded fold-chevron measurement constants** in the marker-repositioning CSS
  (`decorations.ts`) are the one place this design violates its own "read native values
  live" rule — a theme or Obsidian update that resizes the chevron degrades layout
  cosmetically. 5b's session solved the equivalent problem with live measurement; porting
  that approach is a tracked hardening task.
- **CSS guide alignment across themes/fonts** — mitigated in practice (verified across
  bundled light/dark themes and one community theme), but RTL text, IME composition, and a
  wider community-theme sweep remain untested; tracked as hardening/polish.
- **Community CSS snippets/themes overriding our styling** — narrowly scoped `to-` prefixed
  class names, `!important` only where matching Obsidian's own specificity requires it, not
  as a first resort; documented as a known limitation rather than an unbounded specificity
  war.
- **Two documented invariants must survive future refactors**: (a) DOM injection into
  widget-atom subtrees relies on Obsidian never re-diffing those opaque subtrees internally
  — an undocumented-to-Obsidian invariant whose failure mode would be re-injection
  flicker/duplicated markers; (b) never append a child into a plain `.cm-line` — confirmed
  to peg the renderer via CM6's mutation-observer feedback loop. Both are called out
  explicitly here and in `tasks.md` so a future change doesn't rediscover them the hard way.
- **Visual redundancy if obsidian-outliner/obsidian-zoom are also enabled** — unchanged from
  the original proposal, already covered by the existing one-time coexistence warning
  Notice.

## Migration Plan

Unchanged from the original proposal: purely additive UI layer, no data model, no file
content, no plugin-data schema changes beyond the new `markerVisibility` field (which has a
default and degrades gracefully if absent from persisted data). Ships as a normal minor
change: build, unit tests (`tests/decorate.test.ts`), e2e DOM/screenshot coverage
(`e2e/specs/50-decorations.e2e.ts`, `51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts`,
`52-block-markers-icons.e2e.ts`), manual dev-vault visual pass. No rollback concerns beyond
disabling the plugin, which already restores stock rendering.

## Open Questions

- **Shrinking only our own added list margin** — raised during Experiment 1's review, not
  yet decided. List items sit visibly further right than a same-depth sibling
  paragraph/blockquote, traced to Obsidian's own native list hang (`text-indent`/
  `padding-left`) pre-existing in vanilla Obsidian, not something this design introduces —
  but a narrower, still-additive-only fix (reduce only our own added margin by the list
  root's own native hang width, read live) stays open as a possible follow-up. Two risks
  noted but unresolved: clamping so a shallow `supplementalDepth` never goes negative, and
  compensating from the list *root*'s own hang, not each item's (nested items can have
  wider markers with different native hang widths). See doc 10's "Open question" section.
- **Marker/guide interactivity** (click-to-select, click-to-fold, per-heading-level markers)
  remains explicitly deferred, per the Non-Goals above. Doc 10's prior-art addendum
  (obsidian-lapel) records a concrete idea for how a future change might attach this to the
  real DOM widget the marker layer already has, without committing to it here.

Let me quickly review the fixtures and decorate files, then set up the branch and map out the work ahead. I've got a list of tasks to tackle: 4.1, 4.2, 5.1 through 5.6, and 5.8. Task 4.1 is an e2e test checking that nested editor decorations don't have unwanted padding or margins when a table cell is in edit mode, and 4.2 is another e2e test validating the non-mutation contract.

For 5.1, I need to replace the hardcoded chevron constants with live measurements. The current CSS uses fixed values for the transform offset and dead space, but I should measure the actual chevron glyph and wrapper dimensions at render time in MarginCompensation, then set CSS custom properties on a container so the transform can reference them dynamically instead of relying on constants. The chevron only shows up on foldable headings, but the reach applies to all headings via static CSS whenever the chevron exists in the DOM. I need to measure one representative element per render, accounting for cases where the chevron might be hidden with opacity. If there's no chevron present, I'll keep the fallback values instead of updating the custom properties. For protecting these invariants, I could add a regression test since a lint rule would be difficult with DOM manipulation patterns. I think the pragmatic approach is to add an ESLint rule in decorations.ts that flags DOM append operations with a message explaining the invariant, then use inline eslint-disable comments at the two legitimate call sites where we know it's safe—the widget marker prepend and the MarkerWidget's toDOM appendChild.

Now I'm checking how Obsidian's updateOptions works with the editor extensions. When updateOptions is called, it reconfigures all editors by dispatching a reconfigure effect with the current extension array. Since we're reusing the same extension instances, the ViewPlugins should persist rather than get recreated.

The issue is that MarginCompensation relies on the docViewUpdate hook, which only fires when the document view actually changes. A reconfigure transaction that doesn't alter decorations won't trigger docViewUpdate, so updateOptions might not work reliably. I'm considering switching MarginCompensation to use the update hook instead, which fires on every transaction including reconfigure. The tradeoff is that update runs during the update cycle when DOM writes and measurements aren't allowed, whereas docViewUpdate runs after the view has been rendered. So the regression tests already cover the table-only scenario — there's one that checks marker visibility on a table-only note and another that polls for zero markers. I should experiment by swapping out the forceRedraw call with updateOptions() and running those tests locally to see if it works. If it passes, I can replace the hack; if it fails, I'll document why and keep it. The more principled approach is that updateOptions triggers each editor to reconfigure, and since the extension is a closure over the modes object, it'll read the fresh markerVisibility value without needing to swap anything.

But there's a catch with updateOptions — it dispatches reconfigure which runs ViewPlugin.update(), so the DecorationsPlugin and MarkersPlugin will recompute fine, but MarginCompensation.docViewUpdate only fires if the document view actually changed. For table-only notes the decorations stay identical, so there might not be a docView update unless reconfiguration forces a full redraw. Since the ViewPlugin instance stays the same, it probably won't get recreated, which means updateOptions likely won't be enough unless I also modify MarginCompensation to recompute during its update() method when a config change occurs.

The tricky part is that writing DOM in update() violates CM6's rules — I'd need to use requestMeasure with a write phase to schedule the DOM update properly, something like calling apply() within the write phase of requestMeasure. But apply() both reads and writes, and the read phase shouldn't touch layout, so I'd need to be careful about how I structure this.

Actually, docViewUpdate itself is a safe hook for DOM writes, so maybe the simpler path is to try updateOptions() and test it empirically on the table-only scenario. If it fails, I can keep the current hack and document it — the task allows that. I'll implement the experiment and let the tests guide whether I need the hybrid approach.

For consolidating the parse logic, I'm thinking of creating a shared computation function keyed by document identity using a WeakMap cache on the state.doc object, returning all the computed facts, factsByLine, guides, and guidesByLine in one go. Each ViewPlugin would call this function to avoid redundant parsing.

The cache works across updates when the doc text hasn't changed since the computation only depends on the text itself, not the mode — mode gating happens before and marker filtering happens after. This means zero parses on non-doc changes after the first one, and the WeakMap handles memory cleanly. Next I need to look at the Obsidian API docs for the getSettingDefinitions declarative settings API. I need to add the gutter-shift costs to design.md's Risks section since they're not fully documented yet, then update the checklist in doc 10 as tasks complete. For verification, I'm running the plugin build, taking screenshots of the decoration fixtures across both themes using the existing spec test, running full desktop and mobile e2e tests, and ensuring lint passes with zero errors. Then I'll branch off main to start implementation.

I'm thinking through the commit strategy — grouping by task/risk level rather than strict order. The 5.4 consolidation refactor should come first since it has full suite safety, then 5.1 chevron, 5.3 updateOptions eval, 5.5 settings, 5.6 aria plus verification, 5.2 lint guard, and finally 4.1/4.2 tests and 5.8 docs. Tests could actually land earlier since they're independent. I'm also checking what remains unknown about decorate.ts exports, the fixtures file, and obsidian.d definitions.

Now I need to look at the fixtures, decorate.ts, eslint config, and obsidian version to understand getSettingDefinitions and the eslint setup.
I have the full picture now and need to check the fixtures, decorate.ts, the eslint config, and the Obsidian API typings for the declarative settings API.
